name: Blue Book Closing
description: The "Blue Book Closing" refers to the conspiracy theory surrounding the official termination of Project Blue Book, the U.S. Air Force's investigation into Unidentified Flying Objects (UFOs) from 1952 to 1969. The theory posits that the Air Force did not genuinely cease its investigation, but rather moved it underground, concealing evidence of extraterrestrial contact from the public. Proponents argue that the official explanation for the project's closure – that UFOs posed no threat to national security and exhibited no technology beyond existing scientific knowledge – is a deliberate fabrication designed to mask a secret, ongoing study of UFOs and their occupants. Evidence cited includes alleged inconsistencies in official reports, witness testimonies claiming continued government interest in UFO phenomena after 1969, and the purported existence of classified documents detailing advanced, extraterrestrial technology. Mainstream scientific and historical perspectives largely dismiss the "Blue Book Closing" theory, citing a lack of credible evidence and attributing continued UFO sightings to misidentification of conventional objects, psychological phenomena, and hoaxes. Skeptics point to the thoroughness of Project Blue Book's documentation, its open access to the public, and the absence of any convincing evidence of a cover-up. They also emphasize the cognitive biases and psychological factors that can contribute to belief in conspiracy theories. Furthermore, the vast scale of the alleged cover-up, requiring the silence of numerous individuals over decades, is considered highly improbable by many outside the UFO community.
subtitle: The alleged secret continuation of UFO research.

claims.claim: Project Blue Book's closure was a deliberate cover-up.
claims.evidence: Inconsistencies in official reports, witness testimonies, missing documents.
claims.criticism: Lack of verifiable evidence, misinterpretation of data, psychological biases.

claims.claim: The government continues to secretly investigate UFOs and extraterrestrial contact.
claims.evidence: Alleged classified documents, anecdotal accounts of government involvement, unexplained phenomena.
claims.criticism: Absence of credible proof, reliance on unsubstantiated claims, Occam's Razor.

claims.claim: The true purpose of Project Blue Book was to debunk UFO sightings and manage public perception.
claims.evidence: Emphasis on negative findings, dismissive tone in official reports, selective reporting of data.
claims.criticism: Objective analysis of documented cases, lack of resources to properly investigate, genuine uncertainty surrounding many cases.

keyFigures.name: J. Allen Hynek
keyFigures.role: Astronomer and scientific consultant to Project Blue Book. Later became more sympathetic to the idea of UFOs being extraterrestrial.

keyFigures.name: Philip J. Klass
keyFigures.role: UFO skeptic and investigator who frequently debunked UFO sightings and criticized proponents of the extraterrestrial hypothesis.

mythologyConnections: Connections to folklore surrounding fairies and other aerial beings

timeline.event: Project Blue Book is established by the U.S. Air Force.
Project Blue Book is officially terminated.
The "Blue Book Closing" conspiracy theory gains traction within the UFO community.

sources.author: Curtis Peebles
sources.work: Watch the Skies!: A Chronicle of the Flying Saucer Myth
sources.title: Curtis Peebles
